_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: She doesn't have feelings. She has a scoreboard, and she's always counting.

museum_objects.title: Standard & Poor's 500 Index Launch Records
museum_objects.description: Historical records of the launch of the S&P 500, the first computer-calculated stock market index, tracking 425 industrials, 25 railroads, and 60 utilities.
museum_objects.significance: Victoria was the first index built by a machine, not a gut feeling — she was designed from day one to never lie about what the market was actually doing.
museum_objects.plain_language: Historical records of the day in 1957 when the S&P 500 launched as the first stock index calculated by a computer.

display_alternatives.title: S and P 500 daily linear chart 1950 to 2016.png

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: NYSE Building, 8-18 Broad Street
museum_objects.description: The historic New York Stock Exchange building, epicenter of the American equity markets Victoria measures.
museum_objects.significance: Every company Victoria tracks eventually has to answer to this building — a physical monument to the capitalism she keeps score of.
museum_objects.plain_language: The famous New York Stock Exchange building, the physical heart of the American stock market Victoria represents.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.title: Charging Bull Sculpture
museum_objects.description: Arturo Di Modica's iconic bronze bull sculpture, installed near Wall Street after the 1987 Black Monday crash as a symbol of market resilience.
museum_objects.significance: Installed right after a crash, this bull is basically Victoria's own defiant expression — a reminder that she always finds a way to climb back.
museum_objects.plain_language: The famous bronze bull statue near Wall Street, installed after a big market crash to symbolize the market's ability to recover.

museum_objects.title: Smithsonian Stock Ticker Artifact
museum_objects.description: An original mechanical stock ticker machine, the analog ancestor of the real-time data feeds Victoria is built from today.
museum_objects.significance: Before Victoria existed as a number on a screen, this clunky machine was the only way anyone found out how the market was actually doing.
museum_objects.museum: Smithsonian National Museum of American History
museum_objects.plain_language: An old mechanical stock ticker machine, the ancestor of the instant digital stock data we have today.
museum_objects.institution: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

museum_objects.title: Lord & Taylor Gray Flannel Suit (1950)
museum_objects.description: A classic postwar American gray flannel business suit, symbol of the corporate culture that populated the companies in Victoria's index.
museum_objects.significance: This is the uniform of the men who built the companies Victoria measures — buttoned-up, conformist, and utterly convinced the market would only go up.
museum_objects.plain_language: A classic 1950s business suit, the exact style worn by the corporate executives who ran the biggest American companies of the era.

world_map.map_description: Victoria was calculated into existence on Wall Street in 1957 and has since become the world's most-watched benchmark of corporate American performance.

nodes.label: NYSE, Broad Street
nodes.description: The New York Stock Exchange, home to the majority of companies tracked in the S&P 500.

nodes.label: Federal Hall
nodes.description: Historic site adjacent to the NYSE, symbolic of the birth of American federal financial institutions.

nodes.label: Bowling Green (Charging Bull)
nodes.description: Location of the iconic Charging Bull sculpture symbolizing market resilience.

nodes.label: Chicago (CME/CBOE)
nodes.description: Major derivatives markets where S&P 500 futures and options are heavily traded.

nodes.label: Washington, D.C. (SEC)
nodes.description: Home of the Securities and Exchange Commission, which regulates the markets Victoria measures.
